events.text: First, generate a new SSH key on your laptop. Ed25519 is secure, small, and well supported. You do this once; the same key works for every server you add. This is plain SSH, independent of any tooling here.
events.afterText: Key generated. The public half is what we copy to each server.
events.prose: Generate a new SSH key on your laptop. Ed25519 is secure, small, and well supported. Run it once; the same key works for every server. This is plain SSH and has nothing to do with rdc.
events.afterProse: Key generated. The public half is what gets copied to each server.

events.text: Now authorize the key on your first server with ssh-copy-id. It appends your public key to the server's authorized list, so future logins skip the password.
events.afterText: Authorized on the first server. No more password prompts there.
events.prose: Authorize the key on your first server with ssh-copy-id. It appends your public key to the server's authorized_keys so future logins skip the password.

events.text: Do the same for every other server you manage. Here we authorize the second one. Just repeat ssh-copy-id for each machine.
events.afterText: Both servers now trust this key. SSH is fully set up.
events.prose: Repeat ssh-copy-id for every other server you manage. Here we authorize the second one.

narrations.text: Alright! Let's set up SSH keys so you can reach your servers without passwords. This is pure SSH; rdc isn't involved yet.
Two ideas. Generate one key on your laptop, then authorize it on each server with ssh-copy-id. Generate once, copy per server.
Your key is set and your servers trust it. Next up, we add your first server to rdc.
